page.projects.title: Selected Work
page.about.title: About
page.about.subtitle: Data Analyst
page.about.statement: I work with data the way an architect works with space — not to fill it, but to give it structure. Every dataset carries a story that resists easy extraction. The craft is in the listening.
about.t0.title: Cartographic Roots
about.t0.body: Trained in geography and spatial analysis — each map a hypothesis, each dataset a lens.
about.t1.title: From Pixels to Patterns
about.t1.body: Moved from traditional GIS into computational methods. Python became the bridge between spatial intuition and statistical rigor.
about.t2.title: Structured Intelligence
about.t2.body: Working at the intersection of data science and urban systems. The question is never just what the data says — but what it obscures.
about.t3.title: The Adjacent Possible
about.t3.body: Exploring ML for spatial prediction. How can algorithmic systems be made legible and accountable to the communities they describe?

page.connect.subtitle: Open to collaborations and research partnerships.

project.urban-heat.title: Urban Heat Island Mapping
project.urban-heat.insight: Satellite thermal data reveals inequitable heat distribution across urban neighborhoods.
project.urban-heat.problem: Urban heat islands disproportionately affect low-income communities, but most city planning tools lack granular thermal data to guide equitable intervention.
project.urban-heat.method: Processed Landsat 8 thermal imagery using Google Earth Engine and Python. Applied spatial interpolation and overlaid census demographic data to identify vulnerable populations.
project.urban-heat.finding: Neighborhoods with less than 15% tree canopy experienced surface temps 4–8°C higher than adjacent green corridors, strongest in historically redlined areas.
project.urban-heat.impact: Findings informed a city pilot program targeting tree planting and cool-roof installations in three priority zones, projected to reduce peak temps by 2–3°C.
project.transit-access.title: Transit Accessibility Index
project.transit-access.insight: Spatial gaps in public transit create invisible barriers to economic mobility.
project.transit-access.problem: Standard transit metrics measure ridership and on-time performance but fail to capture how well the network connects underserved populations to job centers.
project.transit-access.method: Built a multi-modal accessibility index combining GTFS transit data, OpenStreetMap pedestrian networks, and employment density layers using graph-based shortest-path algorithms.
project.transit-access.finding: 42% of low-income households had commute times exceeding 60 minutes to the nearest employment cluster vs. 11% of median-income households.
project.transit-access.impact: Adopted by the regional transit authority as a supplementary planning metric for route redesign evaluation.
project.land-use-model.title: Predictive Land Use Modeling
project.land-use-model.insight: ML on parcel-level data can anticipate zoning pressure years before rezoning requests.
project.land-use-model.problem: Municipalities react to development pressure rather than anticipating it, leading to fragmented land use decisions and strained infrastructure.
project.land-use-model.method: Trained a gradient-boosted model on 15 years of parcel transactions, permit activity, and proximity features. Validated against held-out rezoning events.
project.land-use-model.finding: Model identified future rezoning hotspots with 78% precision at 65% recall, outperforming heuristic-based forecasts by 2.3×.
project.land-use-model.impact: Deployed as an internal tool for a county planning department to proactively assess infrastructure needs in growth corridors.
project.olist-churn.title: Olist Customer Intelligence & Churn Prevention
project.olist-churn.insight: 97% of customers buy only once — an end-to-end ML pipeline to predict who's about to leave and quantify the cost of letting them go.
project.olist-churn.problem: Olist, Brazil's largest e-commerce marketplace, faces a severe retention crisis: over 97% of customers make a single purchase and never return, leaving substantial lifetime value unrealised. Acquisition-focused metrics masked this structural weakness entirely.
project.olist-churn.method: Built a full customer analytics pipeline on 100K+ transactions across 2016–2018. Steps: RFM segmentation with quintile scoring, K-Means clustering (silhouette-validated, k=4), cohort retention heatmaps, CLV modelling, and a Random Forest churn classifier trained on recency, frequency, monetary value, and delivery experience features. Paired with an interactive A/B test sample size calculator for experiment planning.
project.olist-churn.finding: Recency is the dominant churn predictor. K-Means surfaces 4 behaviorally distinct segments that sharpen the RFM rules. Top 20% of customers drive ~80% of revenue — yet most remain single-purchase buyers with untapped repeat potential. The Random Forest model achieves strong ROC-AUC, identifying at-risk customers before they silently lapse.
project.olist-churn.impact: Targeted retention of high-value at-risk segments projects a 3–5× ROI on campaign spend. The accompanying A/B test calculator operationalises the findings — letting the team size experiments rigorously before launch, turning a one-off analysis into a repeatable retention infrastructure.
